subject.name: Carpentry & Joinery
subject.description: Timber joints, fixings, hand and power tools, setting out.

quizzes.title: Timber Joints
quizzes.description: Name the joint and know where it belongs.

questions.text: Match each joint to its typical application.

pairs.right: Drawer sides — resists being pulled apart
Two rails crossing in the same plane

questions.explain: The dovetail's angled pins mechanically resist tension — that is why drawer fronts use them.

questions.text: In a mortise and tenon, what is the traditional proportion of tenon thickness to timber thickness?
questions.explain: One third keeps the tenon strong while leaving enough cheek either side of the mortise.

questions.text: Order the steps for cutting a through mortise and tenon by hand.
items: Mark the face side and face edge on both members
Set the mortise gauge to the chisel width and mark both pieces
Cut the mortise with a mortise chisel, working from both faces
Saw the tenon cheeks down to the shoulder line
Test fit and pare to a snug fit
Glue, cramp and check for square
questions.explain: Gauge from the face side and face edge every time, or the joint will wind.

questions.text: A bridle joint is essentially the reverse of a mortise and tenon.
questions.explain: The bridle cuts an open slot instead of an enclosed mortise — easier to cut, but weaker in racking.

quizzes.title: Fixings, Fasteners & Power Tool Safety
quizzes.description: The right fixing for the substrate, and staying safe with the tools.

questions.text: Match each fixing to the correct substrate.

pairs.right: Cracked or perforated masonry under high load

questions.explain: Never use a nylon plug in plasterboard — it will pull straight through.

questions.text: Which voltage tool is standard on many UK construction sites for reduced electric shock risk?
questions.explain: 110 V CTE means a maximum of 55 V to earth — survivable where 230 V often is not. Yellow plugs and transformers.

questions.text: Select ALL correct practices when using a circular saw.
options: Let the blade reach full speed before entering the cut
Remove the lower guard for a better view
Check the blade is sharp and correctly fitted
Support the workpiece so the offcut cannot pinch the blade
Carry the saw by its trigger
questions.explain: Removing the guard and carrying by the trigger are how people lose fingers. Pinching causes kickback.

questions.text: What causes kickback on a circular saw?
options: The blade binding or pinching in the cut, throwing the saw back at the operator
Using a blade with too many teeth
questions.explain: A riving knife keeps the kerf open behind the blade specifically to prevent this.

questions.text: A stud wall is 4.8 m long with studs at 400 mm centres, plus a stud at each end. How many studs are needed?
questions.explain: 4800 ÷ 400 = 12 spaces, and spaces + 1 = 13 studs. The classic 'fence post' error is answering 12.
